Implements the core Flask application class, providing WSGI application functionality and request handling.

# Purpose
The code defines a class `Flask` that extends the `App` class, implementing a WSGI application framework. This class serves as the central object for a Flask application, managing the configuration, URL routing, request handling, and response generation. It acts as a registry for view functions, URL rules, and template configurations. The `Flask` class provides methods to handle HTTP requests, manage application and request contexts, and process exceptions. It also includes functionality for serving static files, managing sessions, and creating test clients and CLI runners for testing and command-line interactions.

The `Flask` class is designed to be instantiated with the name of the application package or module, which helps in resource loading and debugging. It includes several configuration options, such as `static_url_path`, `static_folder`, `template_folder`, and others, to customize the behavior of the application. The class also provides methods for creating and managing Jinja environments for template rendering, handling URL building, and managing the lifecycle of requests and application contexts. The `Flask` class is intended to be used as the main application object in a Flask project, providing a comprehensive set of tools and methods for building web applications.

[View Source →](<../../../../src/flask/app.py#L81>)

- **Members**:
    - `default_config`: Holds the default configuration settings for the application.

    - `response_class`: Specifies the class used for response objects.
    - `session_interface`: Defines the session interface to use, defaulting to `SecureCookieSessionInterface`.

- **Description**: Implements a WSGI application and acts as the central object for a Flask application. It serves as a registry for view functions, URL rules, template configuration, and more. The class is initialized with the name of the module or package of the application, which is used to resolve resources. It provides various configuration options and methods to handle requests, manage sessions, and interact with templates and static files.

#### Flask\.\_\_init\_\_<!-- {{#callable:flask/src/flask/app.Flask.__init__}} -->
[View Source →](<../../../../src/flask/app.py#L226>)

Initializes a Flask application instance with configuration options for static files, templates, and other settings.
- **Inputs**:
    - `import_name`: The name of the application package.
    - `static_url_path`: The URL path for serving static files, defaults to the name of the `static_folder`.
    - `static_folder`: The folder containing static files, defaults to 'static'.
    - `static_host`: The host to use for the static route, required if `host_matching` is True.
    - `host_matching`: Boolean indicating if host matching is enabled, defaults to False.
    - `subdomain_matching`: Boolean indicating if subdomain matching is enabled, defaults to False.
    - `template_folder`: The folder containing templates, defaults to 'templates'.
    - `instance_path`: An alternative path for the application instance, defaults to 'instance'.
    - `instance_relative_config`: Boolean indicating if config files are relative to the instance path, defaults to False.
    - `root_path`: The root path of the application files, used when it cannot be detected automatically.
- **Logic and Control Flow**:
    - Calls the parent class [`__init__`](<debughelpers.py.md#debugfileskeyerror__init__>) method with the provided parameters to initialize the application.
    - Creates a Click command group for registering CLI commands and assigns it to `self.cli`.
    - Sets the name of the Click group to the application's name.
    - Checks if the application has a static folder configured.
    - If a static folder is configured, asserts that `static_host` and `host_matching` are compatible.
    - Uses a weak reference to the application instance to avoid reference cycles.
    - Adds a URL rule for serving static files using the [`add_url_rule`](<sansio/scaffold.py.md#scaffoldadd_url_rule>) method.
- **Output**: No return value; it initializes the instance attributes and sets up static file handling and CLI command registration.
- **Functions Called**:
    - [`flask/src/flask/debughelpers.DebugFilesKeyError.__init__`](<debughelpers.py.md#debugfileskeyerror__init__>)
    - [`flask/src/flask/cli.AppGroup`](<cli.py.md#appgroup>)
    - [`flask/src/flask/sansio/scaffold.Scaffold.add_url_rule`](<sansio/scaffold.py.md#scaffoldadd_url_rule>)
    - [`flask/src/flask/blueprints.Blueprint.send_static_file`](<blueprints.py.md#blueprintsend_static_file>)
- **See also**: [`flask/src/flask/app.Flask`](<#flask>)  (Base Class)


---
#### Flask\.get\_send\_file\_max\_age<!-- {{#callable:flask/src/flask/app.Flask.get_send_file_max_age}} -->
[View Source →](<../../../../src/flask/app.py#L281>)

Determines the maximum cache age for a file when using the `send_file` function in a Flask application.
- **Inputs**:
    - `filename`: A string representing the file name or `None`.
- **Logic and Control Flow**:
    - Retrieves the `SEND_FILE_MAX_AGE_DEFAULT` value from the current application's configuration.
    - Checks if the retrieved value is `None` and returns `None` if true, indicating no timed cache.
    - Checks if the value is an instance of `timedelta` and returns the total seconds as an integer if true.
    - Returns the value directly if it is not `None` or a `timedelta`.
- **Output**: An integer representing the maximum cache age in seconds, or `None` if no cache age is set.
- **See also**: [`flask/src/flask/app.Flask`](<#flask>)  (Base Class)


---
#### Flask\.send\_static\_file<!-- {{#callable:flask/src/flask/app.Flask.send_static_file}} -->
[View Source →](<../../../../src/flask/app.py#L308>)

Serves static files from the `static_folder` attribute of the Flask application.
- **Inputs**:
    - `filename`: The name of the file to serve from the static folder.
- **Logic and Control Flow**:
    - Checks if the `static_folder` is set; if not, raises a `RuntimeError`.
    - Calls [`get_send_file_max_age`](<blueprints.py.md#blueprintget_send_file_max_age>) to determine the cache duration for the file.
    - Uses [`send_from_directory`](<helpers.py.md#send_from_directory>) to serve the file from the `static_folder` with the specified cache duration.
- **Output**: A `Response` object representing the served static file.
- **Functions Called**:
    - [`flask/src/flask/blueprints.Blueprint.get_send_file_max_age`](<blueprints.py.md#blueprintget_send_file_max_age>)
    - [`flask/src/flask/helpers.send_from_directory`](<helpers.py.md#send_from_directory>)
- **See also**: [`flask/src/flask/app.Flask`](<#flask>)  (Base Class)


---
#### Flask\.open\_resource<!-- {{#callable:flask/src/flask/app.Flask.open_resource}} -->
[View Source →](<../../../../src/flask/app.py#L330>)

Opens a resource file relative to the application's root path for reading.
- **Inputs**:
    - `resource`: Path to the resource relative to the application's root path.
    - `mode`: Mode to open the file in, with valid values being 'r', 'rt', or 'rb' for reading.
    - `encoding`: Encoding to use when opening the file in text mode, ignored in binary mode.
- **Logic and Control Flow**:
    - Checks if the provided mode is one of 'r', 'rt', or 'rb'; raises a ValueError if not.
    - Joins the root path of the application with the resource path to form the full file path.
    - Opens the file in binary mode if 'rb' is specified, otherwise opens it in text mode with the specified encoding.
- **Output**: Returns a file object opened in the specified mode and encoding.
- **Functions Called**:
    - [`flask/src/flask/testing.FlaskClient.open`](<testing.py.md#flaskclientopen>)
- **See also**: [`flask/src/flask/app.Flask`](<#flask>)  (Base Class)


---
#### Flask\.open\_instance\_resource<!-- {{#callable:flask/src/flask/app.Flask.open_instance_resource}} -->
[View Source →](<../../../../src/flask/app.py#L363>)

Opens a resource file relative to the application's instance folder for reading or writing.
- **Inputs**:
    - `resource`: Path to the resource relative to `instance_path`.
    - `mode`: Mode to open the file in, default is 'rb' (read binary).
    - `encoding`: Encoding to use when opening the file in text mode, default is 'utf-8'. Ignored in binary mode.
- **Logic and Control Flow**:
    - Joins `instance_path` with `resource` to form the full file path.
    - Checks if 'b' is in `mode` to determine if the file should be opened in binary mode.
    - Opens the file in binary mode if 'b' is in `mode`, otherwise opens it in text mode with the specified `encoding`.
- **Output**: Returns a file object opened in the specified mode and encoding.
- **Functions Called**:
    - [`flask/src/flask/testing.FlaskClient.open`](<testing.py.md#flaskclientopen>)
- **See also**: [`flask/src/flask/app.Flask`](<#flask>)  (Base Class)


---
#### Flask\.create\_jinja\_environment<!-- {{#callable:flask/src/flask/app.Flask.create_jinja_environment}} -->
[View Source →](<../../../../src/flask/app.py#L385>)

Creates a Jinja environment with specified options and adds Flask-related globals and filters.
- **Inputs**: None
- **Logic and Control Flow**:
    - Copies `jinja_options` to a new dictionary `options`.
    - Checks if `autoescape` is not in `options`, and if not, sets it to `select_jinja_autoescape`.
    - Checks if `auto_reload` is not in `options`, retrieves `TEMPLATES_AUTO_RELOAD` from the configuration, and sets `auto_reload` accordingly.
    - Creates a Jinja environment using `jinja_environment` with the current instance and `options`.
    - Updates the environment's globals with Flask-related functions and objects like `url_for`, `get_flashed_messages`, `config`, `request`, `session`, and `g`.
    - Sets the `json.dumps_function` policy in the environment to `json.dumps`.
- **Output**: Returns the configured Jinja `Environment` instance.
- **See also**: [`flask/src/flask/app.Flask`](<#flask>)  (Base Class)


---
#### Flask\.create\_url\_adapter<!-- {{#callable:flask/src/flask/app.Flask.create_url_adapter}} -->
[View Source →](<../../../../src/flask/app.py#L425>)

Creates a URL adapter for a given request or application context.
- **Inputs**:
    - `request`: A `Request` object or `None`. If provided, the URL adapter is created for this request.
- **Logic and Control Flow**:
    - If `request` is not `None`, check if `TRUSTED_HOSTS` is set in the configuration and assign it to `request.trusted_hosts` if so.
    - Retrieve the host from the request environment using `get_host` and assign it to `request.host`.
    - Initialize `subdomain` to `None` and retrieve `SERVER_NAME` from the configuration.
    - If `host_matching` is enabled in `url_map`, set `server_name` to `None` to avoid ignoring the actual host.
    - If `subdomain_matching` is not enabled, set `subdomain` to the default subdomain or an empty string.
    - Return a URL adapter bound to the request environment with the specified `server_name` and `subdomain`.
    - If `request` is `None` and `SERVER_NAME` is set in the configuration, return a URL adapter bound to the `SERVER_NAME`, `APPLICATION_ROOT`, and `PREFERRED_URL_SCHEME`.
    - Return `None` if neither `request` is provided nor `SERVER_NAME` is set.
- **Output**: A `MapAdapter` object if a URL adapter is successfully created, otherwise `None`.
- **See also**: [`flask/src/flask/app.Flask`](<#flask>)  (Base Class)


---
#### Flask\.raise\_routing\_exception<!-- {{#callable:flask/src/flask/app.Flask.raise_routing_exception}} -->
[View Source →](<../../../../src/flask/app.py#L478>)

Intercepts routing exceptions and raises a custom exception if certain conditions are met.
- **Inputs**:
    - `request`: A `Request` object that may contain a routing exception.
- **Logic and Control Flow**:
    - Checks if the application is not in debug mode or if the routing exception is not an instance of `RequestRedirect` or if the exception code is 307 or 308 or if the request method is one of 'GET', 'HEAD', or 'OPTIONS'.
    - If any of the above conditions are true, raises the original routing exception.
    - If none of the conditions are true, imports [`FormDataRoutingRedirect`](<debughelpers.py.md#formdataroutingredirect>) and raises it with the request.
- **Output**: Does not return; raises an exception instead.
- **Functions Called**:
    - [`flask/src/flask/debughelpers.FormDataRoutingRedirect`](<debughelpers.py.md#formdataroutingredirect>)
- **See also**: [`flask/src/flask/app.Flask`](<#flask>)  (Base Class)


---
#### Flask\.update\_template\_context<!-- {{#callable:flask/src/flask/app.Flask.update_template_context}} -->
[View Source →](<../../../../src/flask/app.py#L506>)

Updates the template context with commonly used variables and ensures original context values are not overridden by context processors.
- **Inputs**:
    - `context`: A dictionary representing the template context to update with additional variables.
- **Logic and Control Flow**:
    - Initialize `names` with a single `None` value.
    - Check if `request` is available to determine if the template is rendered within a request context.
    - If `request` is available, extend `names` with the reversed list of `request.blueprints`.
    - Create a copy of the original `context` to preserve initial values.
    - Iterate over each `name` in `names`.
    - For each `name`, check if it exists in `self.template_context_processors`.
    - If it exists, iterate over each function in `self.template_context_processors[name]`.
    - For each function, ensure it is synchronous and update `context` with the result of the function call.
    - After processing all context processors, update `context` with the original context values to ensure they are not overridden.
- **Output**: None
- **Functions Called**:
    - [`flask/src/flask/ctx.RequestContext.copy`](<ctx.py.md#requestcontextcopy>)
    - [`flask/src/flask/app.Flask.ensure_sync`](<#flaskensure_sync>)
- **See also**: [`flask/src/flask/app.Flask`](<#flask>)  (Base Class)


---
#### Flask\.make\_shell\_context<!-- {{#callable:flask/src/flask/app.Flask.make_shell_context}} -->
[View Source →](<../../../../src/flask/app.py#L534>)

Provides the shell context for an interactive shell by running all registered shell context processors.
- **Inputs**: None
- **Logic and Control Flow**:
    - Initialize a dictionary `rv` with keys `app` and `g`, where `app` is the current instance and `g` is a global object.
    - Iterate over each processor in `self.shell_context_processors`.
    - For each processor, update the `rv` dictionary with the result of calling the processor.
    - Return the updated `rv` dictionary.
- **Output**: A dictionary containing the shell context for the application.
- **See also**: [`flask/src/flask/app.Flask`](<#flask>)  (Base Class)

#### Flask\.run<!-- {{#callable:flask/src/flask/app.Flask.run}} -->
[View Source →](<../../../../src/flask/app.py#L546>)

Runs the Flask application on a local development server with options for debugging and environment variable loading.
- **Inputs**:
    - `host`: The hostname to listen on, defaulting to '127.0.0.1' or the host in the 'SERVER_NAME' config variable if present.
    - `port`: The port of the webserver, defaulting to 5000 or the port defined in the 'SERVER_NAME' config variable if present.
    - `debug`: A boolean to enable or disable debug mode, which allows automatic reloading and a debugger on exceptions.
    - [`load_dotenv`](<cli.py.md#load_dotenv>): A boolean indicating whether to load environment variables from '.env' and '.flaskenv' files, defaulting to True.
    - `options`: Additional options to be forwarded to the underlying Werkzeug server.
- **Logic and Control Flow**:
    - Checks if the 'FLASK_RUN_FROM_CLI' environment variable is set to 'true' and if not running from a reloader, it ignores the call to prevent blocking the 'flask' CLI command.
    - Loads environment variables from '.env' and '.flaskenv' files if 'load_dotenv' is True and updates the 'debug' flag if 'FLASK_DEBUG' is set in the environment.
    - Overrides the 'debug' flag if the 'debug' parameter is provided.
    - Determines the host and port to use based on the provided parameters or the 'SERVER_NAME' configuration.
    - Sets default options for 'use_reloader', 'use_debugger', and 'threaded' based on the 'debug' flag.
    - Displays the server banner using 'cli.show_server_banner'.
    - Attempts to run the server using 'werkzeug.serving.run_simple' with the specified host, port, and options.
    - Resets the '_got_first_request' flag to False after the server stops.
- **Output**: None
- **Functions Called**:
    - [`flask/src/flask/helpers.get_load_dotenv`](<helpers.py.md#get_load_dotenv>)
    - [`flask/src/flask/cli.load_dotenv`](<cli.py.md#load_dotenv>)
    - [`flask/src/flask/helpers.get_debug_flag`](<helpers.py.md#get_debug_flag>)
    - [`flask/src/flask/cli.show_server_banner`](<cli.py.md#show_server_banner>)
- **See also**: [`flask/src/flask/app.Flask`](<#flask>)  (Base Class)

#### Flask\.test\_client<!-- {{#callable:flask/src/flask/app.Flask.test_client}} -->
[View Source →](<../../../../src/flask/app.py#L669>)

Creates a test client for the Flask application to facilitate unit testing.
- **Inputs**:
    - `use_cookies`: A boolean indicating whether to use cookies in the test client; defaults to True.
    - `kwargs`: Additional keyword arguments that are passed to the test client class constructor.
- **Logic and Control Flow**:
    - Check if `self.test_client_class` is None; if so, import `FlaskClient` from `.testing` and assign it to `cls`.
    - Return an instance of `cls` initialized with `self`, `self.response_class`, `use_cookies`, and any additional keyword arguments.
- **Output**: Returns an instance of `FlaskClient` or a custom test client class if specified.
- **See also**: [`flask/src/flask/app.Flask`](<#flask>)  (Base Class)


---
#### Flask\.test\_cli\_runner<!-- {{#callable:flask/src/flask/app.Flask.test_cli_runner}} -->
[View Source →](<../../../../src/flask/app.py#L727>)

Creates a CLI runner for testing CLI commands in a Flask application.
- **Inputs**:
    - `kwargs`: Additional keyword arguments passed to the CLI runner.
- **Logic and Control Flow**:
    - Assigns `self.test_cli_runner_class` to `cls`.
    - Checks if `cls` is `None`, and if so, imports `FlaskCliRunner` from `.testing` and assigns it to `cls`.
    - Returns an instance of `cls`, passing `self` and `kwargs` as arguments.
- **Output**: An instance of `FlaskCliRunner` or the class specified by `test_cli_runner_class`.
- **See also**: [`flask/src/flask/app.Flask`](<#flask>)  (Base Class)


---
#### Flask\.handle\_http\_exception<!-- {{#callable:flask/src/flask/app.Flask.handle_http_exception}} -->
[View Source →](<../../../../src/flask/app.py#L744>)

Handles HTTP exceptions by invoking registered error handlers or returning the exception as a response.
- **Inputs**:
    - `e`: An instance of `HTTPException` representing the HTTP exception to handle.
- **Logic and Control Flow**:
    - Checks if the exception `e` has no error code and returns it unchanged if true.
    - Checks if the exception `e` is an instance of `RoutingException` and returns it unchanged if true.
    - Finds an error handler for the exception `e` using [`_find_error_handler`](<sansio/app.py.md#app_find_error_handler>) and the current request's blueprints.
    - If no handler is found, returns the exception `e`.
    - If a handler is found, ensures the handler is synchronous using [`ensure_sync`](<#flaskensure_sync>) and calls it with the exception `e`.
- **Output**: Returns either the original `HTTPException` or a response value from the error handler.
- **Functions Called**:
    - [`flask/src/flask/sansio/app.App._find_error_handler`](<sansio/app.py.md#app_find_error_handler>)
    - [`flask/src/flask/app.Flask.ensure_sync`](<#flaskensure_sync>)
- **See also**: [`flask/src/flask/app.Flask`](<#flask>)  (Base Class)


---
#### Flask\.handle\_user\_exception<!-- {{#callable:flask/src/flask/app.Flask.handle_user_exception}} -->
[View Source →](<../../../../src/flask/app.py#L779>)

Handles exceptions by either returning a response or re-raising the exception.
- **Inputs**:
    - `e`: An exception object that needs handling.
- **Logic and Control Flow**:
    - Checks if the exception is a `BadRequestKeyError` and if debug mode is on or `TRAP_BAD_REQUEST_ERRORS` is set, it sets `show_exception` to `True` on the exception.
    - If the exception is an `HTTPException` and [`trap_http_exception`](<sansio/app.py.md#apptrap_http_exception>) returns `False`, it forwards the exception to [`handle_http_exception`](<#flaskhandle_http_exception>).
    - Finds an error handler for the exception using [`_find_error_handler`](<sansio/app.py.md#app_find_error_handler>).
    - If no handler is found, it re-raises the exception.
    - If a handler is found, it ensures the handler is synchronous and calls it with the exception.
- **Output**: Returns an `HTTPException` or a `ResponseReturnValue`.
- **Functions Called**:
    - [`flask/src/flask/sansio/app.App.trap_http_exception`](<sansio/app.py.md#apptrap_http_exception>)
    - [`flask/src/flask/app.Flask.handle_http_exception`](<#flaskhandle_http_exception>)
    - [`flask/src/flask/sansio/app.App._find_error_handler`](<sansio/app.py.md#app_find_error_handler>)
    - [`flask/src/flask/app.Flask.ensure_sync`](<#flaskensure_sync>)
- **See also**: [`flask/src/flask/app.Flask`](<#flask>)  (Base Class)


---
#### Flask\.handle\_exception<!-- {{#callable:flask/src/flask/app.Flask.handle_exception}} -->
[View Source →](<../../../../src/flask/app.py#L811>)

Handles exceptions without an associated error handler, always resulting in a 500 InternalServerError response.
- **Inputs**:
    - `e`: An exception object that needs handling.
- **Logic and Control Flow**:
    - Retrieve the current exception information using `sys.exc_info()`.
    - Send the `got_request_exception` signal with the exception `e`.
    - Check the `PROPAGATE_EXCEPTIONS` configuration to determine if exceptions should be propagated.
    - If propagation is enabled, re-raise the exception if it is the active exception, otherwise raise the passed exception `e`.
    - If propagation is not enabled, log the exception using [`log_exception`](<#flasklog_exception>).
    - Create an `InternalServerError` instance with the original exception `e`.
    - Find an error handler for the `InternalServerError` using [`_find_error_handler`](<sansio/app.py.md#app_find_error_handler>).
    - If a handler is found, call it synchronously with the `InternalServerError` instance.
    - Finalize the request with the `InternalServerError` response, indicating it is from an error handler.
- **Output**: A `Response` object representing a 500 InternalServerError.
- **Functions Called**:
    - [`flask/src/flask/app.Flask.log_exception`](<#flasklog_exception>)
    - [`flask/src/flask/sansio/app.App._find_error_handler`](<sansio/app.py.md#app_find_error_handler>)
    - [`flask/src/flask/app.Flask.ensure_sync`](<#flaskensure_sync>)
    - [`flask/src/flask/app.Flask.finalize_request`](<#flaskfinalize_request>)
- **See also**: [`flask/src/flask/app.Flask`](<#flask>)  (Base Class)


---
#### Flask\.log\_exception<!-- {{#callable:flask/src/flask/app.Flask.log_exception}} -->
[View Source →](<../../../../src/flask/app.py#L864>)

Logs an exception as an error using the logger attribute.
- **Inputs**:
    - `exc_info`: A tuple containing exception type, exception instance, and traceback, or a tuple of three None values.
- **Logic and Control Flow**:
    - Logs an error message using the `logger.error` method.
    - Includes the request path and method in the log message.
    - Passes the `exc_info` to the logger to include exception details.
- **Output**: No return value (None).
- **See also**: [`flask/src/flask/app.Flask`](<#flask>)  (Base Class)


---
#### Flask\.dispatch\_request<!-- {{#callable:flask/src/flask/app.Flask.dispatch_request}} -->
[View Source →](<../../../../src/flask/app.py#L879>)

Matches the URL and returns the return value of the view or error handler for a request.
- **Inputs**: None
- **Logic and Control Flow**:
    - Retrieve the current request from the request context.
    - Check if there is a routing exception in the request; if so, raise the routing exception.
    - Retrieve the URL rule associated with the request.
    - Check if the rule provides automatic options and the request method is 'OPTIONS'; if so, return the default options response.
    - Retrieve the view arguments from the request.
    - Call the view function associated with the endpoint of the rule, passing the view arguments, and return its result.
- **Output**: The return value of the view or error handler, which does not have to be a response object.
- **Functions Called**:
    - [`flask/src/flask/app.Flask.raise_routing_exception`](<#flaskraise_routing_exception>)
    - [`flask/src/flask/app.Flask.make_default_options_response`](<#flaskmake_default_options_response>)
    - [`flask/src/flask/app.Flask.ensure_sync`](<#flaskensure_sync>)
- **See also**: [`flask/src/flask/app.Flask`](<#flask>)  (Base Class)


---
#### Flask\.full\_dispatch\_request<!-- {{#callable:flask/src/flask/app.Flask.full_dispatch_request}} -->
[View Source →](<../../../../src/flask/app.py#L904>)

Handles the full dispatching of a request, including preprocessing, dispatching, and error handling.
- **Inputs**: None
- **Logic and Control Flow**:
    - Sets `_got_first_request` to `True` to indicate the first request has been received.
    - Sends the `request_started` signal with the current instance and an asynchronous wrapper.
    - Calls [`preprocess_request`](<#flaskpreprocess_request>) to perform any necessary preprocessing on the request.
    - If [`preprocess_request`](<#flaskpreprocess_request>) returns `None`, calls [`dispatch_request`](<#flaskdispatch_request>) to handle the request and get a response value.
    - Catches any exceptions that occur during request processing and handles them using [`handle_user_exception`](<#flaskhandle_user_exception>).
    - Calls [`finalize_request`](<#flaskfinalize_request>) with the response value to convert it into a proper response and perform any postprocessing.
- **Output**: Returns a `Response` object after finalizing the request.
- **Functions Called**:
    - [`flask/src/flask/app.Flask.preprocess_request`](<#flaskpreprocess_request>)
    - [`flask/src/flask/app.Flask.dispatch_request`](<#flaskdispatch_request>)
    - [`flask/src/flask/app.Flask.handle_user_exception`](<#flaskhandle_user_exception>)
    - [`flask/src/flask/app.Flask.finalize_request`](<#flaskfinalize_request>)
- **See also**: [`flask/src/flask/app.Flask`](<#flask>)  (Base Class)


---
#### Flask\.finalize\_request<!-- {{#callable:flask/src/flask/app.Flask.finalize_request}} -->
[View Source →](<../../../../src/flask/app.py#L922>)

Finalizes a request by converting a return value into a response and invoking postprocessing functions.
- **Inputs**:
    - `rv`: The return value from a view function, which can be a `ResponseReturnValue` or an `HTTPException`.
    - `from_error_handler`: A boolean flag indicating if the method is called from an error handler, defaulting to `False`.
- **Logic and Control Flow**:
    - Convert the return value `rv` into a `Response` object using [`make_response`](<#flaskmake_response>) method.
    - Attempt to process the response using [`process_response`](<#flaskprocess_response>) method.
    - Send a `request_finished` signal with the processed response.
    - If an exception occurs during processing and `from_error_handler` is `False`, re-raise the exception.
    - If an exception occurs during processing and `from_error_handler` is `True`, log the exception and continue.
- **Output**: Returns a `Response` object after processing the input return value.
- **Functions Called**:
    - [`flask/src/flask/app.Flask.make_response`](<#flaskmake_response>)
    - [`flask/src/flask/app.Flask.process_response`](<#flaskprocess_response>)
- **See also**: [`flask/src/flask/app.Flask`](<#flask>)  (Base Class)


---
#### Flask\.make\_default\_options\_response<!-- {{#callable:flask/src/flask/app.Flask.make_default_options_response}} -->
[View Source →](<../../../../src/flask/app.py#L953>)

Creates a default HTTP OPTIONS response for a request.
- **Inputs**: None
- **Logic and Control Flow**:
    - Retrieves the URL adapter from the request context.
    - Calls the `allowed_methods` method on the adapter to get the allowed HTTP methods for the current request context.
    - Creates a new response object using the `response_class`.
    - Updates the `Allow` header of the response with the allowed methods.
    - Returns the response object.
- **Output**: A `Response` object with the `Allow` header set to the allowed HTTP methods.
- **See also**: [`flask/src/flask/app.Flask`](<#flask>)  (Base Class)


---
#### Flask\.ensure\_sync<!-- {{#callable:flask/src/flask/app.Flask.ensure_sync}} -->
[View Source →](<../../../../src/flask/app.py#L966>)

Ensures a function is synchronous for WSGI workers by returning plain functions as-is and wrapping async functions to run and wait for the response.
- **Inputs**:
    - `func`: A callable object that can be a synchronous or asynchronous function.
- **Logic and Control Flow**:
    - Checks if the input function `func` is a coroutine function using `iscoroutinefunction`.
    - If `func` is a coroutine function, it returns a synchronous version of the function by calling `self.async_to_sync(func)`.
    - If `func` is not a coroutine function, it returns `func` as-is.
- **Output**: A callable object that is guaranteed to be synchronous.
- **Functions Called**:
    - [`flask/src/flask/app.Flask.async_to_sync`](<#flaskasync_to_sync>)
- **See also**: [`flask/src/flask/app.Flask`](<#flask>)  (Base Class)


---
#### Flask\.async\_to\_sync<!-- {{#callable:flask/src/flask/app.Flask.async_to_sync}} -->
[View Source →](<../../../../src/flask/app.py#L980>)

Converts an asynchronous coroutine function into a synchronous callable function.
- **Inputs**:
    - `func`: A callable object that represents a coroutine function, which is an asynchronous function that returns a coroutine.
- **Logic and Control Flow**:
    - Attempts to import the `async_to_sync` function from the `asgiref.sync` module.
    - If the import fails, raises a `RuntimeError` instructing the user to install Flask with the 'async' extra to use async views.
    - Returns the `async_to_sync` function from `asgiref.sync`, passing the input `func` to it.
- **Output**: A synchronous callable function that wraps the provided coroutine function.
- **See also**: [`flask/src/flask/app.Flask`](<#flask>)  (Base Class)


---
#### Flask\.url\_for<!-- {{#callable:flask/src/flask/app.Flask.url_for}} -->
[View Source →](<../../../../src/flask/app.py#L1003>)

Generates a URL for a specified endpoint with optional parameters and values.
- **Inputs**:
    - `endpoint`: The name of the URL rule to generate a URL for, which can be relative to the current blueprint if it starts with a '.'.
    - `_anchor`: An optional string to append as an anchor (e.g., '#anchor') to the URL.
    - `_method`: An optional string specifying the HTTP method to generate the URL for.
    - `_scheme`: An optional string specifying the URL scheme (e.g., 'http', 'https') if the URL is external.
    - `_external`: An optional boolean indicating whether the URL should be external (True) or internal (False). If not specified, defaults to True outside a request and False inside a request unless a scheme is provided.
    - `values`: A dictionary of values to use for the variable parts of the URL rule. Unknown keys are appended as query string arguments.
- **Logic and Control Flow**:
    - Retrieve the current request context using `_cv_request.get(None)`.
    - If a request context exists, get the URL adapter and blueprint name from the request context.
    - If the `endpoint` starts with a '.', prepend the blueprint name to the endpoint if a blueprint is active, otherwise remove the leading '.'.
    - Determine if the URL should be external based on the presence of a scheme or the `_external` parameter.
    - If no request context exists, retrieve the application context using `_cv_app.get(None)` and get or create a URL adapter.
    - Raise a `RuntimeError` if no URL adapter is available and `SERVER_NAME` is not configured.
    - Raise a `ValueError` if `_scheme` is specified but `_external` is False.
    - Call [`inject_url_defaults`](<sansio/app.py.md#appinject_url_defaults>) to modify the `values` dictionary with default values for the endpoint.
    - Attempt to build the URL using the URL adapter's `build` method with the provided parameters.
    - If a `BuildError` occurs, update `values` with the provided parameters and call [`handle_url_build_error`](<sansio/app.py.md#apphandle_url_build_error>).
    - If `_anchor` is provided, append it to the URL after encoding it with `_url_quote`.
- **Output**: Returns a string representing the generated URL.
- **Functions Called**:
    - [`flask/src/flask/app.Flask.create_url_adapter`](<#flaskcreate_url_adapter>)
    - [`flask/src/flask/sansio/app.App.inject_url_defaults`](<sansio/app.py.md#appinject_url_defaults>)
    - [`flask/src/flask/sansio/app.App.handle_url_build_error`](<sansio/app.py.md#apphandle_url_build_error>)
- **See also**: [`flask/src/flask/app.Flask`](<#flask>)  (Base Class)


---
#### Flask\.make\_response<!-- {{#callable:flask/src/flask/app.Flask.make_response}} -->
[View Source →](<../../../../src/flask/app.py#L1129>)

Converts the return value from a view function into a `Response` object.
- **Inputs**:
    - `rv`: The return value from the view function, which must be a valid response type such as `str`, `bytes`, `dict`, `list`, `tuple`, `Response` instance, or a WSGI callable.
- **Logic and Control Flow**:
    - Initialize `status` and `headers` to `None`.
    - Check if `rv` is a tuple and unpack it into `rv`, `status`, and `headers` based on its length.
    - Raise `TypeError` if `rv` is `None`.
    - If `rv` is not an instance of `response_class`, convert it to a `Response` object based on its type.
    - Raise `TypeError` if `rv` is not a valid response type.
    - Cast `rv` to `Response`.
    - Set the `status` of `rv` if `status` is provided.
    - Update `rv` headers with `headers` if `headers` is provided.
    - Return the `rv` as a `Response` object.
- **Output**: A `Response` object that represents the HTTP response.
- **See also**: [`flask/src/flask/app.Flask`](<#flask>)  (Base Class)


---
#### Flask\.preprocess\_request<!-- {{#callable:flask/src/flask/app.Flask.preprocess_request}} -->
[View Source →](<../../../../src/flask/app.py#L1271>)

Preprocesses a request by executing registered URL value preprocessors and before-request functions, stopping further processing if any function returns a non-None value.
- **Inputs**: None
- **Logic and Control Flow**:
    - Retrieve the list of blueprint names from the request, including None for the app-level handlers.
    - Iterate over the blueprint names to call URL value preprocessors registered for each name, passing the request endpoint and view arguments to each preprocessor function.
    - Iterate over the blueprint names again to call before-request functions registered for each name, ensuring each function is synchronous.
    - If a before-request function returns a non-None value, return that value immediately, stopping further request processing.
    - If no before-request function returns a non-None value, return None.
- **Output**: A response return value if a before-request function returns a non-None value, otherwise None.
- **Functions Called**:
    - [`flask/src/flask/app.Flask.ensure_sync`](<#flaskensure_sync>)
- **See also**: [`flask/src/flask/app.Flask`](<#flask>)  (Base Class)


---
#### Flask\.process\_response<!-- {{#callable:flask/src/flask/app.Flask.process_response}} -->
[View Source →](<../../../../src/flask/app.py#L1298>)

Modifies the response object before it is sent to the WSGI server by calling registered after-request functions and saving the session if necessary.
- **Inputs**:
    - `response`: A `Response` object that represents the HTTP response to be processed.
- **Logic and Control Flow**:
    - Retrieve the current request context object using `request_ctx._get_current_object()`.
    - Iterate over functions in `ctx._after_request_functions` and apply each function to the `response` object using [`ensure_sync`](<#flaskensure_sync>).
    - Iterate over blueprint names in `request.blueprints` and `None`, and for each name, if it exists in `self.after_request_funcs`, apply each function in reverse order to the `response` object using [`ensure_sync`](<#flaskensure_sync>).
    - Check if the session is not a null session using `self.session_interface.is_null_session(ctx.session)`. If not, save the session using `self.session_interface.save_session(self, ctx.session, response)`.
- **Output**: Returns a `Response` object, which may be modified by the after-request functions.
- **Functions Called**:
    - [`flask/src/flask/app.Flask.ensure_sync`](<#flaskensure_sync>)
    - [`flask/src/flask/sessions.SessionInterface.is_null_session`](<sessions.py.md#sessioninterfaceis_null_session>)
    - [`flask/src/flask/sessions.SessionInterface.save_session`](<sessions.py.md#sessioninterfacesave_session>)
- **See also**: [`flask/src/flask/app.Flask`](<#flask>)  (Base Class)


---
#### Flask\.do\_teardown\_request<!-- {{#callable:flask/src/flask/app.Flask.do_teardown_request}} -->
[View Source →](<../../../../src/flask/app.py#L1326>)

Executes teardown functions after a request is dispatched and before the request context is popped.
- **Inputs**:
    - `exc`: An optional exception that occurred during request dispatching. If not provided, it defaults to the current exception information.
- **Logic and Control Flow**:
    - Check if the `exc` parameter is `_sentinel`, and if so, set `exc` to the current exception information using `sys.exc_info()`.
    - Iterate over the request's blueprints and `None`, checking if each name has associated teardown request functions.
    - For each teardown function associated with a name, call the function in reverse order with `exc` as the argument, ensuring the function is synchronous using `self.ensure_sync`.
    - Send the `request_tearing_down` signal with `self` and `exc` as arguments, using `self.ensure_sync` to wrap the signal.
- **Output**: None
- **Functions Called**:
    - [`flask/src/flask/app.Flask.ensure_sync`](<#flaskensure_sync>)
- **See also**: [`flask/src/flask/app.Flask`](<#flask>)  (Base Class)


---
#### Flask\.do\_teardown\_appcontext<!-- {{#callable:flask/src/flask/app.Flask.do_teardown_appcontext}} -->
[View Source →](<../../../../src/flask/app.py#L1360>)

Executes teardown functions and sends a signal before the application context is removed.
- **Inputs**:
    - `exc`: An optional exception object, defaulting to a sentinel value, which represents an unhandled exception during request processing.
- **Logic and Control Flow**:
    - Check if the `exc` parameter is the sentinel value, and if so, retrieve the current exception from `sys.exc_info()`.
    - Iterate over the `teardown_appcontext_funcs` list in reverse order and call each function with the `exc` parameter.
    - Send the `appcontext_tearing_down` signal with the current instance and the `exc` parameter.
- **Output**: No return value; the function performs actions related to application context teardown.
- **Functions Called**:
    - [`flask/src/flask/app.Flask.ensure_sync`](<#flaskensure_sync>)
- **See also**: [`flask/src/flask/app.Flask`](<#flask>)  (Base Class)


---
#### Flask\.app\_context<!-- {{#callable:flask/src/flask/app.Flask.app_context}} -->
[View Source →](<../../../../src/flask/app.py#L1386>)

Creates an [`AppContext`](<ctx.py.md#appcontext>) for the Flask application to manage application-level data and configuration.
- **Inputs**: None
- **Logic and Control Flow**:
    - Returns an [`AppContext`](<ctx.py.md#appcontext>) instance initialized with the current application instance.
- **Output**: An [`AppContext`](<ctx.py.md#appcontext>) object that represents the application context for the Flask application.
- **Functions Called**:
    - [`flask/src/flask/ctx.AppContext`](<ctx.py.md#appcontext>)
- **See also**: [`flask/src/flask/app.Flask`](<#flask>)  (Base Class)


---
#### Flask\.request\_context<!-- {{#callable:flask/src/flask/app.Flask.request_context}} -->
[View Source →](<../../../../src/flask/app.py#L1407>)

Creates a [`RequestContext`](<ctx.py.md#requestcontext>) for a given WSGI environment.
- **Inputs**:
    - `environ`: A WSGI environment, which is a dictionary containing CGI-style environment variables.
- **Logic and Control Flow**:
    - Instantiates a [`RequestContext`](<ctx.py.md#requestcontext>) object with the current `Flask` application instance (`self`) and the provided `environ` argument.
    - Returns the created [`RequestContext`](<ctx.py.md#requestcontext>) object.
- **Output**: A [`RequestContext`](<ctx.py.md#requestcontext>) object representing the WSGI environment.
- **Functions Called**:
    - [`flask/src/flask/ctx.RequestContext`](<ctx.py.md#requestcontext>)
- **See also**: [`flask/src/flask/app.Flask`](<#flask>)  (Base Class)


---
#### Flask\.test\_request\_context<!-- {{#callable:flask/src/flask/app.Flask.test_request_context}} -->
[View Source →](<../../../../src/flask/app.py#L1423>)

Creates a `RequestContext` for a WSGI environment using specified values, primarily for testing purposes.
- **Inputs**:
    - `args`: Positional arguments passed to `werkzeug.test.EnvironBuilder`.
    - `kwargs`: Keyword arguments passed to `werkzeug.test.EnvironBuilder`.
- **Logic and Control Flow**:
    - Imports [`EnvironBuilder`](<testing.py.md#environbuilder>) from the `.testing` module.
    - Creates an [`EnvironBuilder`](<testing.py.md#environbuilder>) instance with the provided arguments and keyword arguments.
    - Calls `get_environ` on the [`EnvironBuilder`](<testing.py.md#environbuilder>) instance to create a WSGI environment.
    - Creates a `RequestContext` using the generated WSGI environment.
    - Ensures the [`EnvironBuilder`](<testing.py.md#environbuilder>) instance is closed after use.
- **Output**: Returns a `RequestContext` object for the created WSGI environment.
- **Functions Called**:
    - [`flask/src/flask/testing.EnvironBuilder`](<testing.py.md#environbuilder>)
    - [`flask/src/flask/app.Flask.request_context`](<#flaskrequest_context>)
- **See also**: [`flask/src/flask/app.Flask`](<#flask>)  (Base Class)


---
#### Flask\.wsgi\_app<!-- {{#callable:flask/src/flask/app.Flask.wsgi_app}} -->
[View Source →](<../../../../src/flask/app.py#L1479>)

Implements the core WSGI application logic for handling requests and responses in a Flask application.
- **Inputs**:
    - `environ`: A WSGI environment dictionary containing request data.
    - `start_response`: A callable that starts the HTTP response, accepting a status code, headers, and an optional exception context.
- **Logic and Control Flow**:
    - Create a request context using the provided WSGI environment.
    - Initialize an error variable to None.
    - Push the request context to make it active.
    - Attempt to dispatch the full request and obtain a response.
    - If an exception occurs, handle it and set the error variable.
    - Return the response by calling it with the WSGI environment and start_response callable.
    - In the finally block, check for 'werkzeug.debug.preserve_context' in the environment and preserve the context if present.
    - If an error occurred and should be ignored, reset the error variable to None.
    - Pop the request context, passing any error that occurred.
- **Output**: An iterable of bytes representing the HTTP response body.
- **Functions Called**:
    - [`flask/src/flask/app.Flask.request_context`](<#flaskrequest_context>)
    - [`flask/src/flask/ctx.AppContext.push`](<ctx.py.md#appcontextpush>)

---
#### Flask\.\_\_call\_\_<!-- {{#callable:flask/src/flask/app.Flask.__call__}} -->
[View Source →](<../../../../src/flask/app.py#L1529>)

Handles the WSGI call to the Flask application by invoking the [`wsgi_app`](<#flaskwsgi_app>) method.
- **Inputs**:
    - `environ`: A WSGI environment dictionary that contains request data.
    - `start_response`: A callable that starts the HTTP response, accepting a status code, headers, and an optional exception context.
- **Logic and Control Flow**:
    - Calls the [`wsgi_app`](<#flaskwsgi_app>) method with the provided `environ` and `start_response` arguments.
    - Returns the iterable response generated by [`wsgi_app`](<#flaskwsgi_app>).
- **Output**: An iterable of byte strings that represents the HTTP response body.
- **Functions Called**:
    - [`flask/src/flask/app.Flask.wsgi_app`](<#flaskwsgi_app>)
- **See also**: [`flask/src/flask/app.Flask`](<#flask>)  (Base Class)



# Functions

---
### \_make\_timedelta<!-- {{#callable:flask/src/flask/app._make_timedelta}} -->
[View Source →](<../../../../src/flask/app.py#L74>)

Converts an input value to a `timedelta` object or returns it unchanged if it is already a `timedelta` or `None`.
- **Inputs**:
    - `value`: A value that can be a `timedelta`, an integer, or `None`.
- **Logic and Control Flow**:
    - Check if `value` is `None` or an instance of `timedelta`.
    - If true, return `value` as is.
    - If false, convert `value` to a `timedelta` object using `timedelta(seconds=value)` and return it.
- **Output**: A `timedelta` object if `value` is an integer, the same `timedelta` object if `value` is already a `timedelta`, or `None` if `value` is `None`.
